comments.title: Mental Sheath
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
What exactly is the mental sheath? Is it similar energetically to the sphere of protection? Does it reside in the astral plane? How can it be developed? -Water Rabbit

replies.title: Re: Mental Sheath
replies.content:
The mental sheath is one of the four bodies that human beings have. We've all got material bodies, the familiar body made of flesh and bone; we've all got etheric bodies, which are made of life force and include the meridians that acupuncture works with; we've all got astral bodies, which we experience in dreaming; and we have mental sheaths, which are evolving into full bodies. (A sheath becomes a body when it evolves distinct organs.) Each of these is on its own plane, straightforwardly enough:

Material body --> material plane
Etheric body --> etheric plane
Astral body --> astral plane
Mental body --> mental plane

The mental plane's the plane of the higher functions of mind -- it's not the plane of ordinary mental chatter, which is astral. It's through the mental sheath that we begin to perceive things like meaning, purpose, and value. Human souls differ in how far they've developed the mental sheath; pay attention to how easily a person can handle abstract thinking about meanings, purposes, and values and you can see how far along their mental sheath is. As for developing it, that's what meditation is for, though anything that helps you learn reflective, self-conscious thinking also helps.

replies.title: Re: Mental Sheath
replies.content: If I may; If developing mental level organs let’s you develop a mental body. And intuition is a mental level function/organ. Then developing and using one’s intuition basically gives you a mental level organ, to look around with so to speak. That would be really helpful.

replies.title: Re: Mental Sheath
replies.content: Yes -- and that's a useful way to think about it.

replies.title: Re: Mental Sheath
replies.content: What to do of a person who has a much, much harder time handling abstract concepts is your parent?

replies.title: Re: Mental Sheath
replies.content: Accept that people, including people who you care about, will be at varying places in the process of spiritual evolution, and deal as gracefully as you can with the resulting disagreements. Those can't be avoided -- but you can refuse to let it get in the way of the relationship.

replies.title: Re: Mental Sheath
replies.content: Does a more developed mental sheath correlate to higher intelligence as measured by our current tests?  I know those who have a high I.Q. score but are totally inept at abstract thinking, and I know those with normal I.Q.s who easily get abstract spatial or philosophical concepts.

replies.title: Re: Mental Sheath
replies.content: No. One of the unmentionable secrets of IQ tests is that all they measure is how well you'll score on IQ tests. Nobody's yet come up with a working definition of intelligence, for that matter, so it's not as though there's some recognizable benchmark against which tests can be calibrated! I would suggest, based on my own experiences, that intelligence isn't a single thing -- it's a vague label covering dozens of different mental skills, most of which don't correlate to one another at levels above chance and some of which are actively antithetical to some of the others.

replies.title: Re: Mental Sheath
replies.content:
I would go even further to say that intelligence is a vague label covering dozens of different skills, only some of which are even remotely mental.

Some people are so deeply grounded in the physical plane that they have a special aptitude for sensing what is important, pivotal, or has gravity. Other folks sympathize so overpoweringly with the etheric plane that they are blessed with feeling what is moving, intuited, or fated. Still others are charged so creatively by the astral plane that they are gifted with envisioning what is imagined, possible, or dreamed of.

Depending on how those skills end up manifesting, they may supercharge the tiny end of intelligence that we tend to over focus on, or they may completely cripple it. Excelling at the physical coordination needed for sports is notorious for leaving its beneficiaries academically challenged. The same goes for heightened empathy or advanced talents in daydreaming.

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: Mental Sheath
replies.content: I would agree emphatically with that, and it also cuts the other way. My IQ score is pretty high but there are plenty of skills that are part of "intelligence" in the broadest sense in which I'm way below average; I've come to the conclusion that it's because I'm low on these other skills that I have the particular skills that enable me to score high on the tests.

replies.title: Re: Mental Sheath
replies.content:
That could indeed be a contributing factor to your high test scores, but I wouldn't overlook the benefits that unquantifiable subtle-plane talents may have bestowed on you as well. Having a soothsayer's intuition regarding the unspooling of the Fates' skeins might make the guessing of accurate answers on IQ tests a cinch.

In my case, my teachers were worried that they might have to hold me back in first grade, since I had been spending all my time out on the jungle gym, learning the ins and outs of the physical plane. Weren't they surprised when my awareness of the workings of gravity eventually allowed me to guess the right answer to almost all of their pedantically dull test questions?

What standardized tests are meant to measure remains a quandry. Some of the most talented artists test abysmally low, simply because the tests are so esthetically lacking. Anything that tries to kill off artistic creativity as rigorously as standardized testing does is unlikely to be able to connect with gifted artists. Why would they want to waste their talents on something that stiflingly uninspired?

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: Mental Sheath
replies.content: Professor Jim Flynn, who I understand was the first to debunk IQ, only died in 2020. As I understand, he was kicked out of American universities for such a discovery, winding up in New Zealand. I was fortunate enough to be taught Plato by him. A few years later they dropped the classics to try and get rid of him from political science. Flynn wasn't one for listening to authority, so he reinvented himself as a psychologist and taught pretty much until he died.

replies.title: Re: Mental Sheath
replies.content: Not that I want to skirt meditation, but what are other ways to develop that mental sheath? Things that came to my mind are mathematics, philosophy, ecology (systems thinking?), and I remember that you once responded affirmative to my question if acting could also be helpful, in that it teaches you to detach your self from the roles you play, i.e. your identification with your personality (that is, if you can transfer that skill from your job to your whole life and how you think about yourself). Any others?

replies.title: Re: Mental Sheath
replies.content: Any practice that requires your mind to function in terms of meanings, purposes, and values rather than concrete sensory perceptions will help. One of our culture's great achievements in that context is the modern novel. Good fiction -- the kind that deals in meanings and values, and not just repetitive stroking of emotional reactions -- helps build the mental sheath.

replies.title: Re: Mental Sheath
replies.content:
Weird, I was actually coming on here to ask a question in this realm (currently reading, "The Occult Philosophy workbook"). How do we see someone like Scott Fitzgerald, Charles Bukowski, Hunter Thompson, Ernest Hemmingway, Stephen King or Truman Capote? All were great novelists, all suffered from addiction.

Can one have a more developed mental sheath and at the same time a less developed or damaged etheric body?

AV

replies.title: Re: Mental Sheath
replies.content: Yes. One can also have a fairly well developed mental sheath and end up using drugs to numb the impact of a ferociously corrupt and destructive society.

replies.title: Re: Mental Sheath
replies.content:
I can heartily recommend mathematics: once you get to calculus, it's almost all about dealing with things going on in highly abstract spaces (4+ dimensions, infinitesimal neighborhoods… — did you know that there's a sense in which parallel lines meet at a point at infinity, or that there are algebraic structures called "wheels" where you can divide by zero?), using simple visualizations like graphs as a starting point but then developing the intuition far beyond that.

If you've never had a calculus class (or if you're like me and it made no sense), you might give Silvanus P. Thompson's Calculus Made Easy a try. It's over a century old and in the public domain: https://calculusmadeeasy.org/

I also highly recommend the channel Braver New Math on YouTube. Like Silvaus, his calculus playlists develop the subject using an infinitesimal approach that many people find much easier to digest than the traditional limit-based approach (which he still gets to and is itself an excellent exercise in developing mental faculties).

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Good morning and thank you as always for hosting this space.

I’d like to learn a bit about spellwork and what exactly it is and does compared to ritual. I would guess that it leans more towards practical magic as opposed to the often more theurgic focus of ritual, but apart from that I’ve realized, after more than a year now of studying magic, that I don’t really know what spells actually are and what they’re good for.

I’m keeping in mind Dion Fortune’s description of magic so I know it’s nothing like fantasy fiction, but most of what I encounter of spellwork seems to be treated as little booklets of alleged shortcuts to getting what you want out of life, marketed to teenagers who want to be witches.

On a related note, are you aware of good resources for Tarot spellwork in particular?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What's marketed as "spellwork" these days is a very mixed bag, and those little booklets you mention are representative of much of it. It's not a branch of magic I have any experience in, to be frank, so I may not be the right person to ask! What I've seen of it combines simple symbolic actions with a sort of watered-down New Thought approach to setting intentions, and the results are rarely impressive. I'd be interested if anyone else has had a different experience.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
As far as what's marketed as "spellwork", yeah it's mostly garbage that will generate considerably more blowback and resistance than it will coordinate or organize one's intention into manifesting itself in the wider world. Effective magic is always the result of disciplined practice, not the simple amassing of a grab bag of trendy shortcuts.

As for "spells" and what they may be good for, that makes for a much broader category without clearly delineated boundaries. Spells, along with rituals, prayers, clearings, mantras, healings, and anything else worthy of the name of magic, are attempts to channel life force/willpower in an intended/willed direction. That of course runs into the paradoxical complication of how willpower could be willfully willed about. That's the same dilemma as how some tiny manifestation of the life force might exert an altering pull on the entire life force; likewise, how one's tiny intention could possibly affect divine intention. Oh, will it ever make sense? I guess that really depends on our intentions!

Prayers, mantras, etc. can just as plausibly be defined as spells as not. Saying grace to bless one's meal, cursing at that jerk who just ran a red light, and telling yourself that you're getting free from the straightjacket your parents forced you into can each be considered little spells, with a bare minimum of ritual or paraphernalia. Spells can also be thought of as requiring complex gestures, drawings, and the lighting of stuff on fire. Or spells can be categorized as completely exclusive from rituals. Defined simply as attempts to coordinate one's intentions into manifestation, every action we undertake and every thought that precedes them could be considered spells of a sort.

Then there would be the mass marketing of glitzy schlock at disaffected teenagers. God help them! They’re going to need it.

— Christophe

comments.title: SoP while traveling
comments.content:
In a few days I will be traveling on holiday with family for a week.

I do the SoP early in the morning, in a quiet room before the house gets too busy. That will likely not be possible while I'm away.

My tentative plan is to find whatever peace and quiet I can, and do the ritual entirely in the imagination if needed.

Is this the way to go about it? If not, what are the recommendations?

Thank you for Magic Monday as always.

replies.title: Re: SoP while traveling
replies.content: Yes, you can certainly do that. It's useful, in fact, to do the SoP entirely in the imagination from time to time -- it's a great way to develop you capacities of concentration and visualization.

replies.title: Re: SoP while traveling
replies.content:
I will second our host in doing the SoP in one’s imagination. As it happens I am travelling with a train atm and have just 10 minutes ago done a practical version of the SoP in my imagination, followed by a prayer, and a Ogham divination on a subject I needed consulting for.
Second; I find it convenient to use the bathrooms for privacy when a private room is required. Either at home, when the extended family members are staying around. Or when travelling. Just find a bathroom stall. Just go „relieve“ your spiritual needs :-)
I an dead serious. Ihave used a public university toilet stall, top floor south corner of a building, where few people are even using the one WC, for a full daily SoP + prayer + meditation + Divination when the constraints did not allow otherwise. Just lock the stall. :-)

I do hope this does not trigger any decency rules on the forum.

replies.title: Re: SoP while traveling
replies.content: I've done the same thing with bathrooms!

replies.title: Re: SoP while traveling
replies.content:
There is also tops and bottoms of stairwells.
Boiler rooms, broom closets…

After reading a related thread on a MM years ago I started to think about where to go. I found out that there are a lot of places, even in crowded cities, where one can find 30 minutes of peace and seclusion.

replies.title: Re: SoP while traveling
replies.content: I have as well. And there's another wrinkle, which is to get clear what "imagination"means. In the West we have come to give that word the attribute of "fantasy", but it isn't, not with respect to this stuff. When we work assiduously with these rites entirely in our minds we are operating on them on an energetic level that effects not only our consciousness. Ultimately, not doing these physically but "only" mentally or astrally will be all we have. It's been written elsewhere that the importance of acting on the physical plane with physical gestures and sounds relates to bringing the energies down all the way to the physical level, but that isn't always the end goal.

comments.title: “Projections”; Magical & Otherwise
comments.content:
Good Day, Mr. Greer!
I have a question regarding use of the astral light, as opposed to the projection of thought forms.

I’m not a magician, but I do like studying the theory of magic for my own edification… I’ve been working with what I believe is astral light in relation to development of the emotions or emotional center, within a Gurdjieffian context. I think I have gotten pretty proficient over the last five years in learning to project emotions generated astrally into another person’s awareness (or at least out into the ethers), as well as projecting the “sensation” of astral light to another person’s sensation.

Recently, I have been working on the “intellectual center” also, to a large degree in context of my Tibetan Buddhist faith. Today, I noticed I was able to project some rather palpable thought forms in front of my body, emanating from my “intellectual center” while I was listening to some Bach. I had just yesterday, for the first time, generated a Buddhist deity, using the substance of astral light. It made a big difference in my sadhana, and it might have contributed to this thought projection today.

Gurdjieff is big on using the emotional and intellectual centers together, and I find when I connect the active emotional to the intellectual, that my “thought forms” seem easier to project: the forms being a combination of both emotion and intellect.

I’ve read Lévi, do you think what I’m up to has any relation to what he is describing as high magic?
And are emotional expressions and thought forms two separate magical techniques, that are also sometimes used together?

(P.S. I’ve attempted a blog entry on sensing astral light, within a Gurdjieffian context, that the readership might look at if they like: https://to-be-able.blogspot.com/2025/01/sensing-gurdjieffs-hanbledzoin-re-96.html )

Thanks for everything, Mr. Greer!

replies.title: Re: “Projections”; Magical & Otherwise
replies.content: Interesting. What Lévi means by the label "high magic" is the specific body of tradition of Western occultism he teaches, which is of course neither Fourth Way nor Tibetan Buddhism. While some of the underlying mechanisms are of course the same, it's difficult to make exact comparisons between techniques that rely on very different models of the universe of human experience. In Western occult philosophy, for example, thought-forms and emotional patterns are astral phenomena in form and force aspects respectively, so one normally combines them in practice for best results.

comments.title: Starting a Druid grove
comments.content:
Brother John Michael,

I'm at the point where I think I'll have to leave my masonic lodge. There's very little fraternity nor freemasonry there. Since on the one hand Druidry feels like my spiritual home anyway, on the other hand anti-masonic sentiment seems to be on a rise here (Germany), I feel that it's probably not worth the time nor risk sticking around and working on turning it around (though I have tried many times).

My idea is to start a grove of druids here instead and got your Druid Grove Handbook for that purpose. The plan is to translate the rituals in there and see where it takes me (and some of the brothers who are also dissatisfied).

Two questions about that:

  1. I do like having a male-only space in my life (I'm married and we have three daughters), is it possible to do a male-only grove or would that unbalance the energies in some way?

  2. Do you know anything about the International Grand Lodge of Druidism (IGLD), apparently the successor of the Ancient Order of Druids (AOD)? It and its German offshoot seem quite bland and not what I'm looking for at all (ritual, initiation, nature spirituality, magic optional). However, your book is explicitly for the AODA which seems closely related to the AOD/IGLD. Do you think it would make sense to contact the grand lodge about this idea or are they on a different path?

Thank you for your help!

replies.title: Re: Starting a Druid grove
replies.content:
1) I'm very sorry to hear this, but I understand. Some lodges fail to do what Masonry is there to do, and they can be a real downer.

2) It can be done, and indeed it has been done, but the AODA rituals are not well suited to that. Besides...

3) The IGLD (and the AOD from which it descends) is its own system, with its own rituals and practices. No, it's not an esoteric lodge system, but it may be well suited to your needs for a lodge group that isn't Masonic! You might consider contacting the IGLD and seeing if you can get a grove going where you are, and then practice the magical side of things separately from that. One of the good things about being a Druid is that you can belong to more than one Druid order at the same time!

The AODA and the AOD, by the way, have very little to do with one another historically. It's one of the amusing things about Druid history that so many Druid orders have very similar names even when they have very different traditions and teachings!

replies.content: Is there a recipe for this somewhere? Or is it just a pinch of cayenne in a jar full of salt? My workplace needs this stuff urgently.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Take a glass jar, fill it 3/4 full of rock salt, stir in a teaspoon or more of the hottest red pepper you can find, and then take one or more small nails, bend them using a pair of pliers, and push them down into the salt-pepper mix so they're completely hidden. Leave unlidded in some out-of-the-way place, such as the back of a bottom desk drawer. Check regularly -- when the nails corrode or the salt turns funny colors, you need to replace the contents of the jar. (The salt and pepper should be washed down the drain in plenty of cold water and the nails buried in the soil.)

comments.title: George Winston: Rural Folk Piano
comments.content:
After being exhausted from Winter Field Day with my ham radio club and 21 hours of being out in the cold (though the seafood gumbo I made for the event certainly helped) I tuned in Sunday evening to the local PBS station for an episode of Woodsong's Old Time Radio Hour, out of Lexington, KY. I had just finished a meditation on the nature of the National Soul.. and here comes George Winston onto the piano, playing a rag dedicated to and inspired by Professor Longhair. It was really remarkable to see him play. He played some other numbers from his album "Night" as well on the program. He talked with the host of the show, Michael Johnathon (who I happened to meet at my Great Aunt Lois's funeral in 2023, she was a musician and folk music booster) about his style of piano playing and how it was equally inspired by Ray Manzarek from The Doors and Vince Guaraldi. Winston described his style as "rural folk piano." The whole thing resonated with me after my meditation on national soul material from OPW. May the "rural folk piano" tradition live on.

The episode of Woodsong's can be listened to here: https://exchange.prx.org/pieces/440632?m=false

More about the excellent Woodsong's radio program / live show / PBS television show here:

https://www.woodsongs.com/

I always thought the name for this show was particularly druidy.

Justin Patrick Moore

replies.title: Re: George Winston: Rural Folk Piano
replies.content: I'm delighted to hear this! Winston's one of my favorite pianists and composers, and he's also part of the soundtrack of my life; Sara and I went to a concert of his just before our marriage, back in 1984. It's a source of delight to me that he's still pounding the ivories.

comments.title: More Questions About Zen
comments.content:
Hi JMG-
Last week, I asked you about the Zen SOP. Your response gave me a lot to chew on, and now I have some more questions
1. I've done some research into the concept of sunyata. One Zen teacher stated that it roughly means that things are themselves, empty of any meaning that we assign to it. This is different than the explanation you gave last week, when you said to "Feel yourself to be empty of any inherent existence, and then extend the sense of emptiness outward, dissolving all apparent phenomena into void, so that all that remains of the universe is pure void, and no hostile energies or entities exist because nothing exists."
Unless these are two different ways of trying to explain what is roughly the same concept, a concept which lies just on the edge of human understanding and logic, with words that can at best barely describe the ideas that are trying to be communicated. What are your thoughts on this?
2. If sunyata is the understanding that things are themselves, then how would dissolving all of the meaning from everything around us protect us from negative and hostile forces? Is it because part of their power comes from the meaning which we assign to them?

replies.title: Re: More Questions About Zen
replies.content:
1) Buddhism is much more tolerant of doctrinal differences than most Western religions, and it doesn't surprise me that the concept of Sunyata has many different interpretations! I would describe the insight your Zen teacher gave as an example of tathata, "suchness," rather than sunyata -- but I'm not a Zen master, of course.

2) See my comment above. No, that version of sunyata (or, as I'd say, tathata) won't protect you, which is why I recommended meditating on the other.

replies.title: Re: Ogdoad/8
replies.content: The word "ogdoad" simply means any group of eight. In Druidry, the obvious ogdoad is the cycle of eight festivals around the wheel of the year, each of which is assigned its own deity -- though, Druids being Druids, there's no consensus on which deity goes to which festival! You might also be interested to know that in Martinism, a school of Christian occultism, 8 is the number of the Christ...

"So the hateful and angry soul ends up on the lower astral, in a hellish condition where its hate and anger are mirrored back to it, while the loving and generous soul ends up high up in the astral, where its love and generosity are mirrored back to it.“

As I see it, most of us fall in the space in-between these examples. In which astral layer do we end up? Especially the ones with major incongruencies, like my ex: a bona fide friendly and generous person, even to the point of self-harm, but at the same time a pathological liar (for being unable to look at certain realities of his life).

And where does self-care stand in this model? Is the person with the best karma one who relentlessly does good deeds for others, or is the fact of neglecting one’s own well-being in the process something in the order of the Buddhist poison of ignorance? I’m thinking of less severe addictions, for instance, the kinds that harm no one but yourself.

Thank you as always!

replies.title: Re: Afterlife question
replies.content:
1) The astral plane is a spectrum, and most of it lies between the two extremes I traced out. That's where most of us end up -- dealing with lives that include a mix of positive and negative actions and thus getting a mix of pleasant and unpleasant experiences.

2) It really depends on the details. Every action has its consequences, so someone who was relentlessly good to others but abusive to themselves is going to be reborn in a situation where they have to learn how to be kind to themselves as well.

Agreed, though, on Gibson and Sterling getting a bit stale, though Gibson's "Bridge Trilogy" has some de-industrial-ish type elements and more likable characters than the Neuromancer trilogy, so I ended up liking it more. And The Difference Engine is arguably a better cyberpunk novel than almost any others they wrote, despite (because of?) being "steampunk."

One last thing on cyberpunk (a subgenre I like far more than it likely deserves, mostly due to nostalgia for the time where it seemed like it might be coming true in the cool way, rather than in the crappy way we have now, for a while at least): one of the best analyses of what makes it tick as a subgenre that I've seen is actually in the gamemaster section of the RPG Ex Machina - it tries to get past the set dressing and talk about themes and the like, and then since it's made for RPG gamemasters, gives examples of different ways to hit on that theme from what you see in the classics of the genre and their imitators. Unfortunately, the book's a bit pricey to get paper copies of, and doesn't seem to be available as a .pdf.

Also, if you enjoy noir and like comic books, most everything by Ed Brubaker (writer) and Sean Phillips (artist) is pretty good. The "Criminal" series is at the bleaker "noir"-er end of things, and is well done, but kind of a downer. So far, the "Reckless" series is more "hard-boiled detective," where there's plenty of darkness, but usually glimmers of hope/redemption, so it's a bit more to my taste. Hell, they even have a Lovecraftian series called "Fatale" (though more typically Lovecraftian than our host's works, of course)!

My friend argues that comics are actually an excellent medium for noir, since you can get the kind of atmospheric visuals that make noir movies work so well, while also getting the witty narration and snappy dialogue that make noir novels work so well. I think he's on to something.

Lastly, I'd add encouragement to check out Hammett, Chandler, Mickey Spillane, and the like - even though almost everything in them has become a cliche, they weren't when written, and they're still great.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: occult fiction, Whitehead, etc.
replies.content:
Greetings!

There is an odd Holmes pastiche by Randall Collins (of course, edited by him from a previously lost ms by Dr. John Watson) called "The Case of the Philosophers' Ring" (originally 1978).

In the run-up to WWI, Holmes and Watson go to Cambridge at the request of Bertrand Russell to deal with what may be an attempt to attack Ludwig Wittgenstein. Things quickly spiral out of control.

The cast includes Annie Besant, CW Leadbeater, JM Keynes, Srinivasa Ramanujan, Whitehead, GE Moore, GH Hardy, and various other Cambridge luminaries, the Bloomsbury Circle, and so on. The arch-villain is none other than The Wickedest Man in the World (tm); Wittgenstein almost plays the part of Victor Neuburg. Leila Waddell also does a turn.

It's a light, phantasmagoric tale, Obviously, it's not a roman à clef -- no names are disguised! It doesn't cut very deep, and it's a quick read. And, as with all defeated arch-villains, things are not as obvious as they might seem.

It can probably still be found in public libraries, and it might be worth a look on an inclement afternoon, or a longish bus or train ride. It's also interesting in light of the later career of the author.

LeGrand

It got me thinking about a more serious Holmes pastiche, which I have yet to write, but which takes its starting point from the fact that Holmes and Watson would both be sure to know a certain eminent Freemason, active in London during their time, who spent his working hours as the coroner of northwest London...

comments.title: A firewall of attention
comments.content:
JMG,

I’ve been thinking about mastering the opposites which led to experimenting with an exercise to try and identify the source of thoughts as they pop up. It is proving very interesting.
Somewhat modelled on Mouni Sadhu's concentration exercise (watching the second hand) but rather than keep focus the hand moving, the focus is on watching internal feelings, emotions and thoughts.

Sources so far include:
- environment
- other people doing (or not doing) something
- habitual ingrained patterns
- actual thoughts initiated
- pop-up thoughts relating to something seen, heard or read
- random thoughts with no particular source
- a pattern of thought that repeats itself periodically but seemingly has no plainly obvious source.
The thing is, having set up and starting to try and get it to act like a firewall notification to ‘pay attention’, it is shocking how much runs on automatic (if not paying attention) and if it triggers ‘imagination’ because mind is idling, thoughts can run off at speed if not nipped in the bud.

I can see that journaling might help with ‘deep’ patterns, but do you know of any particular exercise approaches that deal with actively monitoring feelings, thoughts and emotions (beyond the Order of Spiritual Alchemy practices or the like) in ‘realtime’ as they occur?

What I am doing at the moment is a meditation exercise where I am trying to observe from above or outside (so to speak) – watching what comes into mind and then trying to pause that, and trace it back to source.
It has also given me a lot to think about regarding what I project onto the screen vs what gets projected onto the screen from outside. Also made me wonder about what we come into the world with from ancestors!

It is working well so far, but if you have any pointers, those would be much appreciated.
Thank you.

comments.title: I am tired of life
comments.content:
The past three years have been for me an exhausting and frightening gamut of dreadful experiences. To protect myself and family from destitution and homelessness, I strove with might and main to obtain for us a lawyer with whose help to sue our grossly abusive landlords, at a time when there was absolutely zero reason to think that we could obtain legal representation. Yet I succeeded. Subsequently I have spent a year driving us thousands of miles from motel to motel, desperately seeking a home.

At last, three months ago, we found a modest and affordable apartment in a beautiful town. I thought that we could finally enjoy our lives and that I could devote myself to my artistic career and to the book on perspective which I am still under contract to author.

But then, as I have lately made public on JMG’s forums, it became apparent that my sister has gone out of her mind. She is incapable of enjoying the things she used to love. She spends much of her time yelling and screaming. Barring a miracle, the happy life that I thought we could have together is not going to be possible.

During all this time I have consistently applied the ideas of New Thought authors to maintain the most positive state of mind that I possibly can - hitherto, with some success.

But this is too much. This is one giant episode of stress and grief too many. I feel that for years I have moved heaven and earth to obtain for us a decent life. And now this.

Oh, yes - and about twelve weeks ago my best friend died, suddenly and unexpectedly: he who had been my most consistent source of moral support.

It feels like my etheric energy is drained, like my system has been flooded with cortisol continuously for years. I feel that I need some kind of spiritual refreshment, and I don’t know where to find it. Transcendental meditation? Maybe. I don’t know. Most things feel like a chore, and I don’t know where to turn to.

replies.title: Re: I am tired of life
replies.content: I'm very sorry to hear that you're going through so difficult a time. I'd encourage you to get counseling and to make sure you're getting adequate rest and, if you can afford it, regular massage. I know it's no consolation to hear that a lot of other people have been going through horribly difficult times over the last few years, but that's the case -- i.e., it's not just you and you're not alone.

replies.title: Re: Netzach, Hod, Mental and Astral Bodies
replies.content: Okay, the issue here is that the mental plane isn't the plane of the ordinary chattering mind -- that's an astral phenomenon, just as much as the emotions. (Thoughts are the form aspect of the astral, feelings the force aspect.) The mental plane is the plane of meaning, purpose, and value, which can be dimly perceived with thoughts or feelings but exist on a plane above ordinary mental or emotional chatter. Think about the difference between abstract words and their meanings -- that's one way many people can grasp the relation between the astral and mental planes.

comments.title: The exception that (kinda) proves your rule
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I'm writing this after listening to a fascinating few podcasts talking with Chris Bache - a professor emeritus in Philosophy and Religious Studies among other things. He has come to wholeheartedly support reincarnation in a way I think you'd approve (his book that covers this is Lifecycles - Reincarnation and the Web of Life). That's not why I'm writing this, though - he has also written on his 20 year long, 73 high dose LSD sessions based on the methods of Stanislav Grof. On hearing some of his discoveries, which have taken him decades to assimilate, it was amazing to find they fit in so much with occult teachings but particularly your ideas on where humanity is going in the next several hundred years - it's going to be 'a crushing ordeal' but humanity will get through successfully. The giveaway to his authenticity with this is he absolutely does not recommend anyone else do this, not directly because of the direct drug effects, but particularly because of the sheer changes in mind-state he experienced compared with the 'everyday'. He only now recommends to anyone else they only use low doses of certain psychedelics for anyone who is looking at expanding their experience and dissolving the ego - the shattering of time was something he now feels he probably didn't need to do, but he did, and he has a responsibility to share the experience. But he does admit some of his deeper experiences are beyond sharing and he wonders at the value of having blown everything wide open and seeing things 'as time and space' when that was only a temporary experience and he's back 'in carnation'...his words..."A lot of my time these days is spent grounding...cause I spent so many years out in the cosmos it's good to be in touch with the Earth". I guess what also comes across in his exploring is that the divine is here - you don't have to blow the whole thing open and you can't bring much of that experience back 'ahead' of time anyway.
The book is LSD and the Mind of the Universe - Diamonds from Heaven.

I guess the question I'm asking is have you heard of him and would you agree on rare occasion, certain mind altering substances can help enhance understanding and reconnect us, I don't know, mindfully?

comments.title: Inquiry About Potential Side Effects of Golden Dawn Practices
comments.content:
Greetings, Sir,

I hope this message finds you well. I wanted to inquire about a matter that has been on my mind regarding the practices associated with the Golden Dawn. Specifically, is there any legitimate cause for concern about potentially negative side effects from engaging in its traditional work?

I have come across opinions suggesting that practices such as daily LBRPs and the Golden Dawn's egregore might influence practitioners in unintended ways—for instance, by fostering a sense of isolation from the broader world. Bob smith of sixty skills has mentioned this at times.

Would you consider this a valid concern for someone contemplating such practices? Additionally, what are the common long-term effects that the Golden Dawn’s teachings and rituals tend to have on individuals?

Thank you for your time and guidance.

replies.title: Re: Inquiry About Potential Side Effects of Golden Dawn Practices
replies.content:
It's very important to add the Middle Pillar exercise to your daily practice within a few months of starting daily LBRPs. Banishing needs to be balanced by invoking; the LBRP is a banishing, the MP is an invocation, and the two produce a harmonious and very effective basic ritual practice you can do for years with excellent results. It's the people who banish and don't invoke who land in trouble.

Regular practice of GD work has different effects depending on whether or not you go through the temple initiation rituals. Done by themselves, it's a good solid training for the subtle bodies and will tend to give you a stronger will, a more vivid and powerful imagination, and the capacity to make your own choices instead of being swept along with collective consciousness. The Outer Order initiations are also helpful, though you'll want to make sure you take it easy after you receive them, as they can be pretty intense. The Inner Order initiations are another matter; some people have good results from them, but others become out-of-control egomaniacs. This is one of the reasons why I recommend people to do self-initiation in the GD system first, rather than plunging straight into an order with temple initiations.

comments.title: bodies and spirit
comments.content:
Greetings!

My question is about the provenance of a specific set of ideas about the structure of the soul and the reincarnationary process (after death and entering in a new life) that appears in the works of Dion Fortune, Gareth Knight, and others following on the Society of the Inner Light pattern.

Specifically, the ordinary incarnated aspect of the soul, referred to as the "personality", is said to last for an incarnation. At death, it enters into a recapitulation process (compare Yeats' "dreaming back") during which the experiences of the prior incarnation are processed and abstracted. (The pre-sleep daily recapitulation is said to help this process.) Eventually, the Personality is essentially dissolved, with its essential patterns being assimilated into the "Individuality", the reincarnating component, which is successively enriched by the patterns laid down by successive personalities. The Individuality, it seems, follows a similar cycle, contributing to the development of the spiritual monad.

This process is subject to certain vicissitudes, or pathological events, among which are the possible generation of "magical bodies", patterns of stress that become re-animated, often inadvertently, in magical work, but that can also be used as vehicles by inner plane beings to "reach down" the planes to communicate with incarnate persons. (The idea is that reaching down across several planes, from spiritual to material, requires too much energy to be done in one go, and that redeployment of an existing intermediate "body" can help bridge the gap, though it can also lead to distortions.)

Now, some of this clearly derives from Blavatsky, in particular the scheme of planes. But source of the more technical side, some of which appears in the Cosmic Doctrine and some only later, and has to deal with pathologies, abnormalities, and specific technical aspects of communication and transmission between entities on different planes, is a bit less clear.

Would you happen to know whether the specific, technical material appears anywhere else? Perhaps somewhere in Steiner, or Alice Bailey?

Thanks for your help.

LeGrand

comments.title: Occult history - genies/djinni
comments.content:
Recently, I was re-listening to Pogo's Aladdin remix, called Carpet Ride. He's an artist who samples and flips things into a very distinct, catchy style. Anyway, that got me reading into the roots of the Aladdin story, which while stated to be in "a city of China", I believe is set in Central Asia, either in Samarkand or Bukhara -- there are historical reasons for this.

So I'm wondering, what does occult history have to say on the whole thing of rubbing oil lamps (and a ring in the original) and having wishes granted by genies? Presumably, this is tied to the folklore of djinni in the Islamic world (since the words are related), which I believe are astral entities of a fiery disposition, but that's as far as I can get. Any further info would be appreciated.

replies.title: Re: Occult history - genies/djinni
replies.content: All that would have to be followed up in Arab occultism, which is a field I haven't studied at all. Anyone else?

replies.title: Re: Occult history - genies/djinni
replies.content:
I guess you can't be an expert on every topic! Thanks anyway.

btw, the historical reasons I mentioned: the Tang dynasty extended all the way into Central Asia, and some of the khanates there (eg Kara-Khanid) claimed association with China. This explains perfectly why a tale set in "China" is clearly Middle Eastern/Islamic. In addition to Samarkand or Bukhara, the city could've also been Balasagun, Kashgar (which actually is on the far-western end of modern-day China), Paykend, among others.
If you look up those cities, they definitely have the right aesthetic. Also, there's this artifact from the region:
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Lampe_%C3%A0_double_bec_(Paykend).jpg
Other than it having two spouts instead of one, that is an absolute Aladdin-style oil lamp!
Just thought I'd share my findings, if nothing else.

Everybody refused the money, so she left with her friends with the $20.

Since people dropping money like this around me is very rare so far as I know, I was really wondering if there is some interpretation I should be attaching to this, especially since this was my last day of chanting.

2) I chant to a YouTube video with a still of Shani just to get the number of chants correct. Right before doing my chants tonight, I asked Saturn for a clear, unambiguous sign as to whether I should continue or not. Within seconds, the screen blacked out into sleep mode (and this happened twice), which shouldn't happen because of my settings.

3) I interpreted this to mean that this is really my last day of chanting, and then began thinking that the $20 this afternoon "bookends" the original $20 I found on the floor and gave away in December.

OK, so finally getting to the point:

1) Is this a reasonable interpretation? Is there another likely way to interpret this (for example, I hope today doesn't mean that Saturn is just annoyed with me)?

2) If my interpretation is correct, what is the correct way to end this? I used to just meditate daily--do I just return to that? Should I be doing something in between to "close off" this month-long chanting? Do I just start chanting to another God to improve a different aspect of my life (for example, I wish to start a business and to move...)

3) As a more general question, how do I draw the line between seeing meaning in events and just becoming superstitious about everything?


If you made it to the end, thanks again for your time and patience and any/all insights!

replies.title: Re: Saturn Again
replies.content:
1) Yes, it seems reasonable.

2) Just finish the month of chanting and go on to whatever spiritual practice seems productive to you. Saturn doesn't place value in fuss.

3) That's a tough one! The best advice I know of is the old Vietnam adage, "once is chance, twice is coincidence, three times is enemy action." Something needs to repeat itself more than once, or have other really obvious elements of synchronicity surrounding it, before you should pay attention to it.
